title: Polymer solid electrolyte from amorphous poly[epichlorohydrin-co-(ethylene oxide)]/lithium perchlorate complex
abstract:
Polymer solid electrolyte from amorphous
poly[epichlorohydrin-co-(ethylene oxide)]/
lithium perchlorate complex
S Kohjiya,1 T Horiuchi,2 K Miura,3 M Kitagawa,3 T Sakashita,3
Y Matoba3 and Y Ikeda2*
1Institute for Chemical Research, Kyoto University, Uji, Kyoto 611-0011, Japan
2Faculty of Engineering and Design, Kyoto Institute of Technology, Matsugasaki, Kyoto 606-8585, Japan
3Daiso Co Ltd, Otakasu-cho 9, Amagasaki, Hyogo 660-0842, Japan
Abstract: High molecular mass copolymers (P(EH/EO)s) were synthesized by coordination anionic
polymerization of ethylene oxide (EO) and epichlorohydrin (EH), and P(EH/EO)s were used for the
preparation of the ion-conducting matrix. The copolymers having EO unit contents of less than
63mol% were amorphous, and subjected to the complexation with lithium perchlorate. The tem-
perature dependence of ionic conductivity of the P(EH/EO)/LiClO4 complexes was expressed by the
Williams±Landel±Ferry equation. Optimal conductivity was observed as a function of salt concentra-
tion. This is the result of two contradictory factors: the increase of glass transition temperature
(negative for the ionic conductivity) and the increase of carrier number (positive for the ionic con-
ductivity) with increasing lithium perchlorate concentration. The ionic conductivity strongly
depended on EO unit contents in the copolymers. The introduction of EO units to poly(epichloro-
hydrin) main chain increased the ionic conductivity as did the addition of 20wt% poly(oxyethylene)
glycol monomethylether of molecular mass 750.
# 2000 Society of Chemical Industry
Keywords: poly[epichlorohydrin-co-(ethylene oxide)]; high molecular mass; ionic conductivity; lithium
perchlorate; polymer solid electrolyte; amorphous
INTRODUCTION
The need for solid state electrolytes has been increas-
ing because of the requirement for less ecologically
harmful materials.1±3 Among many materials, much
attention has been focused on an elastomeric solid
electrolyte as a polymer solid electrolyte, because
suitably elastic material can give a ¯at, thin and ¯exible
solid electrolyte.4 Elasticity can also give excellent
contact between an electrolytic layer and an electrode
in chemical batteries.
The ionic conductivity s is generally de®ned by
 
X
j
qjnjj
1
where several kinds of ions are present and j denotes
the jth ion. Therefore, s is a summation of the con-
tribution from all kinds of ions; qj is the charge number
of an ion, being an elementary electronic charge e in
the case of a univalent ion; nj is the carrier number; and
mj is the mobility of ions. Equation (1) suggests that the
mobility of the carriers (ions) is the most important
factor for ionic conduction, because ions are much
larger than electrons. The rubbery state is actually a
liquid from the rheological viewpoint, even though it is
classi®ed as a solid from its appearance.5 An elastomer
may therefore be regarded as a polymeric `solvent' and
higher conductivity of ions would be expected in the
matrix.
The authors have been working on elastomeric
materials
for
a
polymer
solid
electrolyte
using
poly(oxyethylene) (PEO) segment as one component
in
the
polymers.
For
example,
PEO-grafted
poly(siloxane),6 segmented polyurethanes with PEO±
poly(oxytetramethylene)±PEO
soft
segments,7
co-
polymeric gels composed of poly(dimethylsiloxane)
and PEO,8 and high molecular mass comb-shaped
PEO with oxyethylene segments as side chains9,10
were studied as elastomeric solid electrolytes. More-
over, we reported the ionic conductivity of hydrin
rubbers.11 Our studies were based on the concept that
the expansion of rubbery PEO phases should increase
ionic conductivity in a manner similar to that reported
in many studies.1±3,12±15
To date, several reports on the ionic conductivity of
hydrin
rubbers,
ie
poly(epichlorohydrin)
(PEH),
poly[epichlorohydrin-co-(ethylene
oxide)]
(P(EH/
EO)) and poly[epichlorohydrin-co-(ethylene oxide)-
co-(allyl glycidyl ether)] have been published.16±22
Recently, the transference number of the hydrin
(Received 1 March 1999; revised version received 3 August 1999; accepted 26 October 1999)
* Correspondence to: Y Ikeda, Faculty of Engineering and Design, Kyoto Institute of Technology, Matsugasaki, Kyoto 606-8585, Japan
# 2000 Society of Chemical Industry. Polym Int 0959±8103/2000/$17.50
197
Polymer International
Polym Int 49:197±202 (2000)
 rubbers/KOH system was reported.23 From the view-
points of excellent heat resistance, good mechanical
properties and good ionic conductivity, hydrin rubbers
are expected to be a suitable material for a solid elec-
trolyte. Additionally, hydrin rubbers have the advan-
tages that the monomers for the polymerization are
commercially available and the synthetic method has
already been established. However, most samples
studied were commercial ones and the composition
of oxyethylene (EO) and epichlorohydrin (EH) was
®xed, ie [EH]/[EO]1/1.
Florjanczyk et al 17 reported the suitability of EO for
providing
excellent
ionic
conductivity
in
the
P(EH/EO)/NaI system, having synthesized several
P(EH/EO)s. However, the effect of salt concentration
on ionic conductivity has not yet been studied.
Additionally,
the
results
of
differential
scanning
calorimetry (DSC) have not been reported in detail.
Thus, in this study high molecular mass amorphous
P(EH/EO)s have been synthesized using a Vanden-
berg catalyst, and their ionic conductivities after
doping with LiClO4 were investigated from the view-
point of the salt concentration. The study of the P(EH/
EO)s doped with LiClO4 should be useful for the
material design of polymer solid electrolyte, because
LiClO4 is one of the most widely used salts in this ®eld.
The effect of the EO unit content of the main chain on
ionic conductivity is also compared with that of
blending oligomeric PEO with the samples.
EXPERIMENTAL
Synthesis and molecular characterization of
P(EH/EO)s
High molecular mass P(EH/EO)s, as illustrated in Fig.
1, were synthesized from EO and EH. Vandenberg
catalyst (a condensation product of dibutyltin oxide
and tributylphosphate (1/2)) was used as a highly
active catalyst for the ring-opening polymerization in
hexane at 20°C.24,25 Florjanczyk and co-workers16,17
used the catalytic system of triethylaluminium/diethyl
ether/water for the preparation of P(EH/EO)s. This
catalyst, however, is not so stable in air, whereas the
Vandenberg catalyst has the advantage that it can be
stored in air for a long time. The copolymers obtained
in this study were puri®ed by precipitation from the
tetrahydrofuran (THF) solutions of copolymers using
methanol
four
times,
and
dried
under
reduced
pressure.
Size exclusion chromatography (SEC) measure-
ments
were
performed on a Shimadzu
C-R4A/
LC6AD/RID-6A instrument using dimethylforma-
mide with 0.01mol lÿ1 LiBr as an elution solvent at
60°C; the weight-average molecular mass Mw and the
number-average molecular mass Mn were calculated
based on the calibration of columns using polystyrene
standards. Four columns of Shodex (KD-807, 806,
806M and 803) were used. An elemental analysis
forC, H and Cl atoms was performed in the Elemental
Analytical Centre of Kyoto University.
The chemical structure was identi®ed by NMR
measurement at 100°C on a Jeol-JNM-GSX-270
spectrometer of Nihon Denshi Co Ltd. Deuterated
dimethyl sulfoxide was used as a solvent. d (ppm):
methine carbons of EH units 77.70, 77.82, 78.10 and
78.24; chloromethyl carbons of EH units 43.70, 43.77
and 43.85; methylene carbons of EH units 68.57±
68.82 (several peaks); methylene carbons of EO units
69.39±70.11 (eleven peaks).
Preparation of polymer solid electrolytes
Homopolymer of EH was commercially available
(Epichlomer H from Daiso Co Ltd); it was used after
reprecipitation four times from the THF solution of
polymer
using
methanol.
Poly(oxyethylene)glycol
monomethylether (PEGM) of molecular mass 750
was supplied by Aldrich Co Ltd.
P(EH/EO)s, PEH and PEGM were dried under
high vacuum at 60°C to constant weight before the
preparation
of
the
samples.
The
reagent
grade
anhydrous lithium perchlorate was obtained from
Mituwa Chemicals Co and used after drying under
high vacuum at 60°C for 48h. THF was re¯uxed over
lithium aluminium hydride and distilled just before
use. Both the matrix polymer and lithium perchlorate
were dissolved in THF, and the solutions were poured
into Te¯on1 moulds to prepare the polymeric elec-
trolyte ®lms. The ®lms were subjected to drying at
room temperature for 5 days, followed by the removal
of solvent under a high vacuum at 60°C for 7 days in
the desiccator.
The amount of lithium perchlorate doped in the
polymers is expressed by the molar ratio of salt against
ether oxygen in the polymer, ie [Li]/[-O-]; this is
displayed in the sample code as a percentage, being the
®rst number for PEH and the second number for
P(EH/EO)s.
The
®rst
number
in
the
code
of
P(EH/EO)s gives the percentage content of EO units
in the main chain.
Characterization of polymer solid electrolytes
Ionic conductivity was determined by complex im-
pedance measurements with platinum blocking elec-
trodes
using
a
computer-controlled
Yokogawa±
Hewlett-Packard 4192 LF impedance analyser. The
polymer electrolyte ®lm, whose thickness was about
0.1±0.7mm, was cut into a disk of 10mm diameter
and sandwiched between platinum electrodes. The
assembly was sealed into a Te¯on1 container and was
placed in a temperature-controlled box. All proce-
dures were carried out under dried nitrogen of high

Characterization of polymer solid electrolytes
Ionic conductivity was determined by complex im-
pedance measurements with platinum blocking elec-
Hewlett-Packard 4192 LF impedance analyser. The
polymer electrolyte ®lm, whose thickness was about
0.1±0.7mm, was cut into a disk of 10mm diameter
and sandwiched between platinum electrodes. The
assembly was sealed into a Te¯on1 container and was
placed in a temperature-controlled box. All proce-
dures were carried out under dried nitrogen of high
Figure 1. Chemical structure of P(EH/EO).
purity (99.9997%). Then, the polymer solid electro-
lyte was subjected to the complex impedance measure-
ment from 20 to 80°C after the thermal equilibrium
had been reached at each temperature. An alternating
current over the frequency range 5Hz to 1MHz was
used, and the applied voltage was 0.5V. The bulk
resistance (Rb) was determined from the complex
impedance (Cole±Cole) plot and the ionic conductiv-
ity was calculated from eqn (2).
where d is the thickness and A is the area of the ®lm.
Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) measure-
ments were performed on a Seiko DSC 20. The
sample was cooled to about ÿ110°C and was then
heated to 100°C at a rate of 10°Cminÿ1.
Preparation of polymer solid electrolytes
The results of the molecular characterization of
P(EH/EO)s are summarized in Table 1 together with
those of PEH. Three kinds of high molecular mass
amorphous copolymers could be prepared by using a
Vandenberg-type catalyst. The Mw and Mw/Mn values
of P(EH/EO)s were of the order of 106molgÿ1 and
about 4.6±5.1, respectively. The compositions of
copolymers were determined by elemental analysis
and 1H NMR spectroscopy. Both results were in good
agreement, and the compositions were EH/EO=76/
24, 52/48 and 37/63. Because of the entanglement of
the long chains of P(EH/EO)s, the self-standing
elastomeric ®lms without crosslinking could be pre-
pared by casting from the THF solutions of copoly-
Effect of EO unit content on Tg of P(EH/EO) ﬁlms and
The amorphous state is accepted to be one of the
important factors for ionic conduction. Therefore, it is
necessary to investigate the effect of EO unit content
on the glass transition temperature (Tg) of P(EH/EO)
®lms with and without salt. In Table 2 are collected the
Tg values of the samples studied in this report, all of
which were obtained from DSC measurements. The
Tg was determined in the midpoint of the Tg curves.
Melting was not detected for all samples. In Fig 2, the
relationship between the EO unit content in the main
chain and the Tg of each sample is illustrated with the
results of ionic conductivity at 30°C. It is notable that
Table 1. Properties of polyethers
Composition of polyethers b[EH]:[EO]
b Measured by 1H NMR and elemental analysis.
Table 2. Results of DSC measurement and the
ionic conductivity of the polyether ﬁlms with and
Amorphous poly[epichlorohydrin-co-(ethylene oxide)] for solid electrolyte
the increase of EO unit content brought about a
decrease of Tg of P(EH/EO) ®lms, both with and
without the salt. The amorphous P(EH/EO) with low
Tg is preferable as a matrix for ionic conduction.
Additionally, it was also observed in Fig 2 that the Tg
of non-doped ®lms decreased linearly with increasing
of the EO unit content. This could be evidence that the
randomness of the copolymers was high. At each salt
concentration, the Tg value of salt-doped ®lm also
content until this reached 48mol%. This suggests that
the samples with 63mol% EO unit content possess the
very ¯exible EO phases to give a low Tg even after the
Effects of salt concentration on Tg of
The doping of inorganic salt increased the Tg except in
the case of PEH as shown in Table 2. Because the Li
interacts with the ether oxygens of EO segments, the
increase is understandable. The observed decrease of
Tg for PEH doped with the salt can be explained by the
presence of chlorine atom in the repeating units of
PEH, which is considered to prevent complexation
between the lithium cation and the ether oxygens of
the backbone chain of PEH. Consequently, the
dissolved salt might result in the decrease of the
intermolecular interaction of PEH, ie the salt seems to
have a plasticizing effect for the EPH matrix.
Figure 3 shows the effect of salt concentration on
the Tg values together with the ionic conductivity
P(EH/EO)-24 and P(EH/EO)-48 increased almost
linearly with the increase of salt concentration in the
range of this study. However, P(EH/EO)-63 gave a
different result, ie the Tg of P(EH/EO)-63-3 was
relatively very low. The ¯exible EO segments in P(EH/
EO)-63-3 seem to remain after adding the salt at this
Effect of EO unit content on ionic conductivity
Figure 4 shows an example of the impedance plots
obtained for the samples prepared in this study. Here,
Z' and Z@ are the real and imaginary parts of complex
impedance, respectively. The ionic conductivity was
calculated from the bulk resistance (Rb) shown in the
®gure. The conductivity of P(EH/EO)-63-5 measured
by the direct current (DC) method was below
10ÿ12Scmÿ1. Therefore, the contribution of electron
conduction was negligible compared with ionic con-
duction. The other samples used in this study showed
pro®les similar to that of P(EH/EO)-63-5.
Figure 2. Effect of EO unit content on Tg and ionic conductivity. *, 0mol%
salt; &, 1mol% salt; ~, 3mol% salt; , 5mol% salt.
Figure 3. Effect of salt concentration on Tg and ionic conductivity: (a) P(EH/EO)-24; (b) P(EH/EO)-48; (c) P(EH/EO)-63.
The ionic conductivities measured at 30 and 60°C
are summarized in Table 2. Figure 2 shows the
relationship between the EO unit content in the main
chain and the ionic conductivity at 30°C, together
with the Tg values. Among four polymer matrixes with
the different EO unit contents, P(EH/EO)-63 dis-
played the highest ionic conductivity at each salt
concentration. The reduction in Tg and the increase in
the polarity both contributed to the increase in
conductivity. The former increased the mobility of
dissociation of salt. Consequently, the migration of
the ion became easier in the matrix with the high EO
unit contents studied in this report.
The temperature dependencies of ionic conductivity
for the samples doped with the salt are shown in Fig. 5.
The Williams±Landel±Ferry (WLF) equation26
where s(T) is conductivity at temperature T, and C1
and C2 are constants, reasonably explains every
behaviour in Fig. 5. The solid lines in Fig 5 are
calculated according to eqn (3). C1 and C2 values of
the samples are summarized in Table 2. They were not
very different from universal constants (C1=17.4 and
C2=51.6)26 which were determined by the rheological
method. The good ®tting in the WLF equation means
that the ionic conduction in these polyethers is
governed by the diffusion in the polymeric rubbery
matrix, and the free volume is the determining factor
for ionic conduction. Interestingly, the temperature
dependence of ionic conductivity was observed to
become small with the increase of EO content. This
phenomenon is also desirable as a matrix of solid
Effect of salt concentration on ionic conductivity
Figure 3 shows the salt concentration dependence of
Tg and ionic conductivity. With increasing salt con-
centration, the Tg of the polymer matrix increased; in
particular, the Tg increased monotonously in the cases
of P(EH/EO)-24 and P(EH/EO)-48 due to the com-
plexation of ions with EO segments. For P(EH/EO)s,
the adequate concentration for the highest ionic con-
ductivity was observed at [Li]/[-O-]=0.03 except for
P(EH/EO)-48 at 20°C. The maximum resulted from
the compensation of two factors, the increase of carrier
number and the increase of Tg brought about by
increasing the salt concentration in a manner similar to
the results of many studies using PEO. It is noted that
the maximum appeared at relatively lower concentra-
Comparison between the effects of added PEGM and
EO segments in the main chain on ionic conductivity
P(EH/EO)-63 as a matrix for ionic conduction,
samples added with oligomeric PEO were prepared
and their ionic conductivities were measured. PEGM,
the molecular mass of which was 750, was used in this
study, and was added to the P(EH/EO)-48/lithium
perchlorate complexes. The amount of PEGM was
20wt%. Thus, the overall EO unit content of the
P(EH/EO)±48/PEGM systems was about 60mol%.
Figure 6 shows the ionic conductivity of P(EH/EO)±
48/PEGM systems in the form of Arrhenius plots. The
curves are all convex and followed the WLF depen-
Figure 5. Arrhenius plots of the ionic conductivity for the samples doped
with LiClO4: M, PEH-1; F, PEH-5; N, P(EH/EO)-24-1; y, P(EH/EO)-24-3;
E, P(EH/EO)-24-5; *, P(EH/EO)-48-1; ~, P(EH/EO)-48-3; &, P(EH/EO)-
48-5; *, P(EH/EO)-63-1; ~, P(EH/EO)-63-3; &, P(EH/EO)-63-5.
Figure 4. Complex impedance plot at 20°C for P(EH/EO)-63-5.
Amorphous poly[epichlorohydrin-co-(ethylene oxide)] for solid electrolyte
dency of conductivity. The sample doped with the salt
at a concentration of [Li]/[-O-]=0.01 displayed the
highest ionic conductivity at 30°C after adding 20wt%
PEGM. The ionic conductivities at 30 and 60°C are
summarized in Table 2. It is clearly seen that the
conductivity increased upon the addition of PEGM.
P(EH/EO)-63 system in the presence of an adequate
salt concentration were comparable with those of the
Generally, the addition of low molecular mass of
PEO always resulted in a reduction of Tg, and this
phenomenon brought about the increase of ionic
conductivity.27 In this study, the Tg of P(EH/EO)-48
was also decreased by adding 20% PEGM of molecu-
lar mass 750. However, the increase of Tg brought
about by increasing the salt concentration was larger in
the P(EH/EO)-48/PEGM system than in P(EH/EO)-
63. This means the segmental motion of the latter was
more ¯exible than that of the former when the salt was
doped at 3 and 5mol%. The phenomenon may be
explained by the different degree of complexation
between the ether oxygens and Li, ie it is considered
that the PEO segments presented in the main chain
were dif®cult to complex tightly with Li because of
steric hindrance. This results in the lowering of Tg for
P(EH/EO)-63, and consequently the ionic conductiv-
ity of the P(EH/EO)-63 system becomes comparable
with that of the P(EH/EO)-48/PEGM system. Thus,
the amorphous P(EH/EO)-63 is concluded to be an
excellent rubbery matrix for the solid electrolyte.
